data.title: It's A Bear Market Rally, The Selloff To Resume
data.description:
The bear market bounce

Technically, we are not in a bear market, but the current bounce looks like a bear market rally.

In fact, the major indices have been in a correction territory, 9% for S&P500 (SP500) and 14% for Nasdaq 100 (QQQ), going towards the technical 20% bear market threshold, but that selloff has been temporarily interrupted with a face-ripping rally to new all-time highs.

Furthermore, these "face-ripping" rallies usually occur during the bear markets and are usually led by the heavily shorted, fundamentally challenged stocks. That's exactly what happened during the April rally - it was led by software stocks (IGV), which face difficult fundamentals given the AI disruption.

Fundamentally, valuations are on par with the dot-com bubble. We are facing a severely adverse scenario - inflationary shock, which is likely to produce demand destruction and a recession. With respect to systemic credit risk, we are facing a private credit crisis, with significant bank exposure.

On top of everything, GDP is already slowing down to a crawl, only 0.5% in Q4 2025 and estimated at 1.3% for Q1 2026, driven mostly by AI investment - while the AI capex has an unknown ROI, which increases the bust risks. At the same time, inflation has been sticky and elevated even before the energy price shock.

Put it all together: fundamentals are deteriorating, valuations are extreme, there is an early-stage credit event, and there is an unfolding major inflationary geopolitical shock - that's a bear market environment.

So, this is nothing new; everybody is aware of these fundamentals - and that's a problem. Logically, given the current environment, an active trader would short the market. This is even more obvious if there is an unfolding largest energy price shock ever, with no end in sight.

Well, this only "end in sight" is the political narrative that "the end is in sight".

So, now combine the massive short positions (in reaction to the fundamentals) with the narrative that "the war in Iran will end soon" and "oil prices will fall sharply" - and you get a massive short-covering rally, driven by the heavily shorted stocks.

That's what happened, in my opinion, and I have been warning that it is dangerous to short stocks in the current environment.

Here is the chart of the S&P 500 with a face-ripping vertical rally in April.

The Iran war

Obviously, the key event moving the market now is the Iran war. The Strait of Hormuz is closed, and oil has been trading near $100/barrel. If this continues for a month or two, we are likely to get closer to the severely adverse scenario, defined by the Fed and the IMF as the inflationary led recession.

So, the rally has been triggered by the expectations of the ceasefire, and accelerated with the actual 2-week ceasefire and further accelerated with the expectations of a possible deal before the ceasefire expires.

The logic is that the deal will cause the price of oil to fall, and thus the inflationary shock will diminish, which will allow the Fed to continue cutting.

This is only a hope, as it will be difficult to make a compromise where Iran gets to retain the threshold state status and some nuclear leverage, while Israel demands no nuclear activity at all. The US would have to make significant long-term security guarantees with significant financial rewards for Iran, which is also unlikely.

Thus, there will likely be a ceasefire extension, but the Strait of Hormuz is likely to be at least partially obstructed indefinitely, which points to a more persistent inflation, and thus a constrained Fed.

The Software stocks

The rally has been led by software stocks up by 14%, where Oracle (ORCL) bounced by almost 30%, and Microsoft (MSFT) bounced by almost 15%. Note, Microsoft accounts for almost 5% of the S&P 500 Index (SPY).

Software stocks are disrupted by AI adoption - nothing fundamentally changed in April, and nothing positive will change anytime soon, AI tools are disrupting the fundamentals of the software industry, so this is not a fundamental rally.

The private credit crisis is directly related to the software industry, and thus alternative managers are also vulnerable. During the current bounce, Blue Owl (OWL) spiked by 20% - again nothing fundamental about the rally either.

The Mag 7 stocks

The S&P 500 can't rally without the Mag 7 stocks, given their weight in the Index. Overall, Mag 7 ETF (MAGS) rallied by almost 18%.

However, the Mag 7 stocks are out of favor now. The AI capex is massive, but without the guaranteed ROI, there are massive obstacles like energy costs, and most importantly, their collective cash flows are declining and debt is increasing. While, their collective accounting profits are still strong, under the surface the problems are emerging.

The valuation

The Shiller PE ratio for the S&P 500 is at 40.67, just below the record valuation during the dot-com era. So, based on this measure, it's a bubble.

Implications

Based on the Shiller P/E ratio, the S&P 500 is a bubble. But, bubbles need a trigger to burst. The currently unfolding inflationary shock could be the trigger that bursts the bubble. Rising inflation constrains the Fed, and tightens credit together with the private credit crisis, which causes demand destructions - and the end result is a bear market with a bubble bust and a systemic credit event. That's basically a bear market on par with 2008, over a 50% drawdown.

The counterargument is that President Trump will not allow this to happen - any time that market starts pricing an adverse scenario, Trump jawbones the market with a positive narrative, and even backs down from some policies. The result is a market bounce due to short covering, and speculative buying.

This positive narrative can continue to affect the market as long as the data stays positive. Once inflation data starts showing persistent pressures and companies start warning about margin pressures, the narrative will become less effective - that's when the market selloff accelerates.

Most imminently, we are facing a ceasefire deadline next week, and in all likelihood the ceasefire will be extended, but this will not alleviate inflationary pressures as long as there is no comprehensive peace deal.

However, even with the deal, investors are facing issues with the tech sector (due to AI disruption and AI capex) and the financial sector (due to private credit).

In my opinion, the peace deal is unlikely, and oil is likely to remain near $100 for the foreseeable future, and we are likely to revisit the lows near 6300 for the S&P 500 as inflation continues to rise during the summer, before the selloff continues to the 20% bear market threshold and exceeds it.

data.title: Quality As A Foundation In An Uncertain World
data.description:
By Ayush Babel and Hyun Kang

Investors are navigating an increasingly complex market backdrop. Geopolitical tensions, ongoing conflicts and energy supply risks continue to add volatility, while rapid technological change, particularly the acceleration of artificial intelligence, is reshaping industries, business models, and sources of competitive advantage.

Against this backdrop of geopolitical uncertainty and technological disruption, identifying companies with durable business models and resilient fundamentals has become increasingly important.

Historically, companies with strong profitability, balance sheet strength, and disciplined capital allocation—characteristics commonly associated with the quality factor—have demonstrated some of the most consistent performance across market cycles. Compared with other equity factors, quality has tended to outperform more frequently over long investment horizons while experiencing relatively shallow periods of underperformance.

Quality Has Shown the Greatest Resilience, Outperforming More Often Than Other Factors

Figure 1: Rolling 10-Year outperformance of U.S. Factors (vs. Market)

Figure 2: Factor Performance Summary

Sources: WisdomTree, Ken French, data as of November 28, 2025. Value: High 30% Book to Price portfolio. Size: Low 30% portfolio. Quality: High 30% portfolio. Low Vol: Low 20% portfolio. High Div: High 30% portfolio. "&" Market: all CRSP firms incorporated in the U.S. and listed on the NYSE, AMEX or NASDAQ. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

The importance of quality becomes even clearer when examining how profitability interacts with valuation and growth characteristics.

Outperformance of Quality Across the Value-Growth Spectrum

Figure 3: Returns by Profitability Across Value and Growth Segments

Source: Kenneth French Data Library, 6/30/63-2/28/2026. Period based on availability of annual operating profitability returns sorted into quintiles, which begins 6/30/1963. Market is U.S.-listed equities grouped on the basis of operating profitability and price-to-book. Returns are annualized. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Companies with higher operating profitability have historically outperformed lower-quality peers across the entire growth-value spectrum, with the strongest effect observed among blend stocks. In other words, sustainable growth is often supported by strong fundamentals.

The WisdomTree Quality Dividend Growth Approach

The WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth Fund (DGRW) seeks to capture this quality premium through a forward-looking approach to dividend investing.

Rather than selecting companies purely on the basis of high dividend yields, the strategy emphasizes firms with the potential to grow their dividends over time. The process focuses on companies with strong profitability metrics and earnings growth characteristics that can support sustainable dividend growth.

The strategy also differs from traditional market capitalization-weighted approaches by weighting companies according to the dividends they pay. This dividend-weighted methodology helps reduce concentration in the largest companies and links portfolio weights more closely to the cash returned to shareholders.

By combining quality screens with a forward-looking dividend growth framework, the strategy aims to identify companies capable of delivering both growing income and long-term capital appreciation.

Conclusion: Exposure Rooted in Fundamentals

Ultimately, the case for quality is not just theoretical—it is clearly reflected in portfolio fundamentals. As shown in the chart below, the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Dividend Growth Index exhibits meaningfully higher profitability metrics than the S&P 500 Index, with return on equity and return on assets notably elevated. At the same time, this higher quality exposure is not achieved at the expense of excessive valuation, with forward price-to-earnings (P/E) broadly in line with the market and only a modest premium on price-to-book (P/B).

Figure 4: Fundamental Comparison

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet. Data as of 3/31/2026.

Importantly, the strategy also delivers a higher forward dividend yield, reinforcing the idea that investors do not need to sacrifice income to gain exposure to stronger businesses. Instead, they can access a combination of profitability, sustainable growth and income generation within a single framework.

In an uncertain and rapidly evolving market environment, this balance becomes increasingly valuable. WisdomTree’s U.S. Quality Dividend Growth strategy provides an approach rooted in fundamentals, one that seeks to capture resilient earnings, disciplined capital allocation, and growing shareholder returns. For investors looking to navigate volatility while maintaining long-term return potential, anchoring portfolios in high-quality businesses may offer a more consistent and dependable path forward.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Growth stocks, as a group, may be out of favor with the market and underperform value stocks or the overall equity market. Growth stocks are generally more sensitive to market movements than other types of stocks. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time. The Fund is non-diversified, as a result, changes in the market value of a single security could cause greater fluctuations in the value of Fund shares than would occur in a diversified fund. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index. The composition of the Index is governed by an Index Committee and the Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Ayush Babel, Director, Quantitative Research

Ayush Babel is the Director of Quantitative Research in WisdomTree's multi-asset quantitative research and index teams. In this role, he focuses on developing innovative quantitative strategies across various asset classes while supporting WisdomTree's diverse range of products. His expertise spans factor exploration, portfolio construction and optimization, quantitative investment research, and product development.

With over a decade of experience in the financial services industry, Ayush has held investment research roles at J.P. Morgan and Franklin Templeton. At these institutions, he was responsible for developing and managing equity and fixed income smart beta products, as well as cross-asset risk premia solutions for global institutional and retail clients. His experience covers a broad spectrum of asset classes and investment styles.

Ayush holds a bachelor's in Engineering Physics and a master’s degree in Nanoscience from the Indian Institute of Technology, Bombay.

Hyun Kang, Research Analyst

Hyun Kang joined WisdomTree in July 2022 as a Research Analyst. As a part of the Index team, he assists with the creation and maintenance of the firm’s indexes and supports the group’s research initiatives across various strategies. Hyun graduated from Carnegie Mellon University, with a B.S. in Business Administration and an additional major in Statistics and Machine Learning.

Original Post

data.title: J.B. Hunt Transport Services: Valuation Seems Rich At The Current Level
data.description:
Investment action

I had a hold rating for J.B. Hunt Transport Services, Inc. (JBHT) previously, as I expected the lack of volume recovery to continue weighing on valuation multiples despite the cost-reduction story playing out nicely. The fundamental setup has changed. Q1 2026 finally showed real revenue growth, better Intermodal trends, and early signs that the freight market is improving. More importantly, JBHT is entering this phase with a leaner cost base, which should help operating leverage if the recovery continues. However, valuation has gone up quite significantly too. At the current multiple, I don't see much of an upside unless JBHT shows really strong adj. EPS growth.

1Q26 earnings review

JBHT's Q1 2026 gave a pleasant surprise on revenue growth. Total operating revenue was ~$3.06 billion, up ~5.1% y/y. Even after stripping out fuel surcharge, operating revenue was still $2.64 billion, up 3% y/y. The segment picture was better too. Intermodal revenue was ~$1.51 billion, up ~2.4% y/y. Loads increased 2.9% y/y, while revenue per load moderated slightly by 0.4% y/y. Management also said intermodal volumes were up 3% y/y; Dedicated revenue was up 2.2% y/y to $841 million; ICS revenue growth was even more solid at 20.4% y/y, driving total revenue to $323 million; Truckload [JBT] revenue did well too at $205 million, up 23.3% y/y; and lastly, Final Mile revenue was the only weak segment that saw negative growth of 6.3% y/y, down to $188 million. As for profitability, EBIT grew 15.9% y/y to $207 million, and the operating ratio [OR] improved to 93.2% from 93.9% a year ago. By segments, Intermodal EBIT grew to $114 million; Dedicated stayed steady at $87 million; while the weaker spots were Integrated Capacity Solutions [ICS], where it saw -$5 million EBIT. Overall, net income still grew to $142 million, and diluted EPS grew to $1.49 from $1.17.

Revenue growth is finally here

Previously, my view was that JBHT was executing well, but the broader freight market was still too weak to support real top-line growth. At that time, I believed my argument made sense since revenue was flat and volumes were mixed. This is no longer the case. Revenue grew. Intermodal volumes grew. JBT revenue grew sharply. ICS revenue grew sharply too. In my view, these are the first signs of a market that is getting better. Of all this, perhaps Intermodal is the best evidence we need. Recall back in Q3 2025, Transcon was down 6%, and total JBI volumes were down 1%. This time, management talked about record first-quarter volume, a record week in March, broad-based strength across the network, and Eastern load growth of ~7%. Even Transcon was no longer in decline (was roughly flat this quarter).

Management's tone also changed for the better. The key message I took away was that management said the freight environment felt meaningfully different from what they had seen over the last few years, with tighter capacity and early demand improvement. Of course, we cannot just trust management's words alone, but if we combine that with the latest quarter's better volume, better revenue, and better segment trends, I believe it has changed the debate. We are starting to see the first signs of recovery, and if this trend continues, the stock should start to price in a recovery ahead.

The fundamental setup leans bullish

Demand gradually recovering is definitely good for the equity story, but it gets better when we consider that JBHT kept getting leaner. Management said in the call that JBHT saved another $30 million of annual costs in Q1, taking annual cost savings to a little over $130 million (this is above the prior $100 million target). The context shifted for the better because those savings are no longer seen as actions to protect earnings in a soft market. Now they do more than that. If revenue is starting to move again, a lower cost base should mean better operating leverage from here, which means we could see a structurally lower OR and faster earnings growth.

We have other data points that support a bullish setup too. For instance, in Dedicated, while the quarter itself was solid, the more important point is what it says about demand. Management said interest in the service is picking up, and JBHT has sold 295 trucks in Q1 while staying on track for 800 to 1,000 new trucks for 2026. This is a very bullish data point because it says customers are feeling the tightness in capacity and are turning to JBHT for solutions. There is some lag here because these new deals have start-up costs hitting the P&L before we see impact at the EBIT line. But this is really not a bad problem to have, as it usually means demand is coming in.

Even the weaker segments also point to an early recovery cycle. It is true that ICS still lost money, and JBT's gross profit was pressured by higher fuel costs and tighter capacity, which pushed up purchased transportation costs. But the point is these do not suggest weak demand. The problem is that costs went up faster than what they could charge customers. Think of it like this: these businesses are paying more to move the freight, but they have not fully raised their prices yet. That can happen early in a recovery. In a weak market, ICS and JBT usually struggle because there is not enough freight to move. Here, the issue looks different. There seems to be freight demand, but profits are being squeezed because costs moved first and pricing is still catching up. What JBHT needs to do here is to start raising prices to reflect the current demand/supply situation, which I don't doubt is doable if we go into an upcycle.

Valuation

In the past 2 decades, JBHT has seen strong adj. EPS growth during the first few years of every cycle recovery (ranging from 20+% to even 50+%). Consensus is currently pricing 20%+ adj. EPS growth over the next two years, which I think could be too conservative if we see a strong recovery, given JBHT's operating cost is lower today.

I think the market has run ahead to start pricing in a recovery. We can see this in the forward PE multiple, now at ~30.5x, which is way ahead of its past 10-year trading history, and adj. EPS growth to be >20% for the next two years. Like I have shown previously, this level of adj. EPS growth is not surprising, and there could be upside considering the lower cost today. Consensus estimates have also been fairly accurate (<4% range on average) over the years. My view on valuing such cyclical companies is to adopt a through-cycle valuation methodology. The question to ask is, "What multiple would JBHT trade in a normalized environment?". Let's assume that FY2029 is where things start to normalize; at that point, JBHT should trade at its historical average (my benchmark for "through-cycle"), which is ~21.7x. Using a consensus estimate of ~$11.44 for FY2029 adj. EPS and multiplying that by 21.7x, I get to a share price target of $248, which is really not that far from where JBHT is today.

For investors to see any meaningful upside here, JBHT has to beat consensus estimates and show a much stronger adj. EPS growth. If not, I think valuation is going to stay capped at this multiple.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating to JBHT. The recovery cycle seems to be happening. At the same time, JBHT is still benefiting from structural cost savings, which should support stronger earnings growth if the cycle keeps improving. There are still areas that need better pricing, but I think the direction of travel has clearly turned more favorable. However, valuation has run ahead and is not cheap if we assume multiples to normalize back to historical average.

data.title: Has Gold's Performance Structurally Changed?
data.description:
Has gold’s volatility persistently increased? - Key drivers behind gold’s rising volatility

Gold’s volatility has picked up in 2026 (Chart 1). It breached its historical upper quartile, rising to the top fifth percentile of the data series since 1971. The drivers of such large swings in the gold price are discussed in our Gold Market Commentary from January, February and March, and include:

  • Cooling Fed rate cut expectations and upticks in bond yields sparked by various triggers such as the announcement of Kevin Warsh as Fed Chair nominee in late January and the Middle East conflict that pushed up inflationary concerns in late February
  • A strengthening of the USD, reversing a three-month declining trend
  • Investor unwinding of long positions in futures, options and gold ETFs following the final exponential surge in gold’s rally, which took it from US$5,000/oz to US$5,500/oz in just three days
  • Stop-loss orders, which amplified gold’s moves when it breached down through key thresholds.

Chart 1: Gold’s volatility has picked up in the beginning of 2026, akin to the spikes seen in previous periods of risk

Gold’s monthly realised volatility since 1971*

It is important to note that gold is not the only asset whose volatility has increased in 2026 (Chart 2). Volatilities of equities and bonds have increased sizably in March. And such episodes have happened before. For instance, during the Global Financial Crisis (GFC), investors sold gold, given its ample liquidity conditions and prior robust performance, to meet other margin calls or liquidity needs. Similar actions were seen when the COVID-19 pandemic took its toll on global financial markets. In most of these incidents, gold has done well and helped investors accumulate “emergency funding sources.” And gold also delivered robust returns when liquidity crunches were over. This is one of the key edges shaping gold’s strategic status in investors’ portfolios: the liquidity source during market stress.

Chart 2: Gold’s historical volatility surges were not isolated

Annualised cross-asset monthly volatility*

Our analysis shows that gold’s volatility is mean-reverting (Chart 3). As shown in Chart 1, gold’s annualised volatility has generally remained between 10% and 18% during most days. Furthermore, historical data suggests a volatility ‘half-life’ (the time it takes for a volatility shock event’s impact to halve) of around 1.6 months, similar to that of equities. The implication is clear: while gold volatility can surge to levels unseen for years, it has historically reverted towards its long‑run norm.

Chart 3: Historical data suggests gold volatility shocks halve after 1.6 months

Left: Decay of a shock’s impact on gold’s volatility; Right: Historical patterns following a volatility shock*

Was gold market liquidity impacted by sell-offs?

Gold trading activity surged sharply during recent market sell-offs, underscoring its deep liquidity in periods of stress (Chart 4). During the last week of January, as the gold price pulled back, average daily gold trading volumes across major venues reached US$965bn/day, or 5,805t/day – the highest level on record. Over-the-counter (OTC) activity, driven largely by LBMA members, averaged US$395bn/day, up 41% w/w. Volumes on major exchanges jumped 45% to US$520bn/day, led by strong increases on COMEX and the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE), while gold ETF trading surged 137% w/w to US$49bn/day.

A similar pattern emerged in March. As gold prices corrected, average daily trading volumes rose to US$525bn/day, up 11% m/m and 46% above the 2025 average of US$361bn/day, with LBMA OTC and COMEX activity particularly strong. This mirrored activity seen in March 2020 when the COVID‑19 pandemic hit global markets and triggered selloffs, global gold trading volumes spiked, reinforcing gold’s role in providing deep liquidity during periods of broad financial stress.

Chart 4: Gold market liquidity ample during recent pullbacks

Gold market trading volumes across different segments*

Chart 5: Spread widening has been episodic, short-lived, and mostly outside standard trading hours

Time-weighted average bid-ask spread in bps*

Chart 6: Gold bid-ask spreads per unit of volatility have fallen from prior stress peaks

Time-weighted average bid-ask spread relative to 30-day realised volatility*

Is gold still the strategic asset for portfolios?

Despite recent volatility spikes, gold remains a strategic asset in investors’ portfolios. Inflation shocks typically lead to positive bond-equity correlations due to their adverse effects on both asset classes. And the recent spike in oil prices linked to the Iran conflict will likely reinforce inflation-related volatility. Meanwhile, gold maintains its low-negative correlation with risk assets, offering investors a safe haven (Chart 7).

Chart 7: Gold’s correlation with equities has been consistently low

Left: bond-equity correlation; Right: gold-equity correlation*

Chart 8: Adding gold can reduce overall portfolio volatility without significantly increasing the risk budget due to its low correlation to equities and bonds

A typical 60/40 global portfolio without gold and with 5% gold between January 2025 and April 2026*

data.title: American Express' Moat Is More Resistant Than What You Think
data.description:
Intro

American Express Company (AXP) has had a rough couple of months, declining 9% in 6 months. With many short-term forces acting against AXP, such as Trump’s intention to cap credit card interest rates at 10% and the oil shock caused by the war, it may provide a nice opportunity to buy based on long-term fundamentals. Since his tweet, Trump has not posted anything else about it, and considering the massive hassle to pass such an act and the massive impact it will have on the economy, I highly doubt this cap will happen. Although there has been a significant de-escalation of the war, there is no certainty of a definitive ceasefire, and the oil shock can reduce spending and even trigger a recession. Out of all of the issuers, I believe AXP is the most resistant,, as its cardholders are generally more financially able to withstand financial pressure. I also believe that in the long term, a recession will not cause AXP to lose cardholders and all of the advantages that come with it. In this article, I will detail its sustainability by explaining its moat, covering its recent changes, and discussing how industry threats can affect AXP in the long term.

Business overview

AXP operates under a closed-loop system, which means that AXP is the network, the issuer, and, in many cases, the acquirer all at once. Although AXP does not enjoy the margins that Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) have, it has an edge in the data it collects. It can collect data on who spent how much, where, and what merchant received it. While owning a network is an incredible business, AXP’s real moat is the customer relationships it has with high-net-worth customers. These cardholders typically spend 50% more, pay their loans faster, and are a key reason why merchants accept paying a higher rate per transaction to Amex. The average proprietary basic card member spend has been steadily increasing at 5% annually over the past 6 years, reaching $25,000 in 2025. This high-spending, creditworthy cardholder base is an irreplaceable moat that Amex has achieved after decades of outstanding service. Amex has ranked at the top of J.D. Power’s credit card satisfaction survey for six consecutive years.

Amex’s service culture dates back a century, when it was one of the few companies that honored letters of credit for Americans stranded in Europe during World War 1. This DNA persists in concierge services that secure impossible restaurant reservations or help cardholders stranded abroad. Customers pay $500–$700 in annual fees to access Amex’s corporate partnerships, direct airline/hotel relationships, integrated travel platform, and fraud detection software.

Over the past seven years, AXP has had a meaningful shift in its revenue mix as net interest income grew from $7.7B (19% of revenue) to $17.4B (24%). This means that AXP is pivoting toward increasing its loan balances, which rose from $82B to $146B. I see two possible explanations for this shift: it may mean that AXP is taking on higher risk to gain market share or prioritize growth; alternatively, it may mean that its closed-loop data advantage has enabled it to issue more loans without taking on higher risk.

AXP’s moat is not the network; rather, it is its control over its ecosystem and the customers it has, and I see many opportunities in this.

Opportunities

I have already touched on the data advantage it has from its closed-loop system. In an open-loop system like Visa’s, the data is split between banks, which collect customer data; Visa, which collects network data; and the acquirer, which collects merchant data. AXP has a full picture of this system, which gives it a massive advantage. This allows AXP to assign custom credit facilities, improve fraud detection, and improve targeting. Targeting is what allows AXP to provide the best service, as it customizes offers, rewards, and services to individual cardholders. Because of the customized experience that Amex is able to provide, I believe it makes it more resilient to macroeconomic factors and new frictionless methods of transacting. Cardholders have every incentive to continue enjoying its rewards and services in the long term and, as explained before, are generally more financially able to withstand an economic downturn.

Because Amex’s moat is not the network but its cardholders, the company is more resistant to Real-Time Payment (RTP) systems and other services that fundamentally challenge the moat of its competitors. I go into detail about RTP systems and the threat posed to network companies in my Visa article; here I will give a quick explanation. RTP systems are created by central banks or by companies in partnership with central banks in order to facilitate instantaneous money transfers between banks. These systems are entirely different networks from Visa’s and Mastercard’s and involve fewer intermediaries, are instant, and are simpler to use. The most advanced RTP system is Brazil's PIX, and it now processes more transactions than Visa and Mastercard combined. One last thing that makes RTP systems inevitable is that they allow central banks to have more control over transactions (more taxes), and by digitizing payments, it removes the central bank’s hassle to print and manage cash. This is why I think eventually every country will have its own RTP systems, which will make credit card networks like VisaNet only used for international transactions.

In the case of Amex, because it is a whole ecosystem where customers get great service, offers, and rewards, they have an incentive to use Amex, and because these cardholders spend 50% more than regular customers, merchants still have an incentive to accept Amex, even though they have to pay a much higher fee than under practically free RTP systems. Amex has 120M cardholders worldwide, which is nothing compared to Visa's 4 billion and Mastercard’s 3 billion. This means that Amex was not maximizing its transaction volume but rather reaching high-quality clients.

When assessing whether Amex is able to withstand the increased amount of loans, I find that overall it is in a good position. Its delinquency rates have stayed roughly where they were in 2018 at a manageable level of 1.4%, and while small business loan delinquency rates are higher at 1.6%, it is not a concern.

AXP holds 2.8x in reserves for every $1 of delinquent balances, which is higher than it was in 2018.

Its net interest income has significantly outpaced losses every single year. In 2025, AXP earned $4.5 of interest income for every $1 of write-offs, which is higher than its pre-COVID levels

Despite intending to reach more small businesses and being more open with third parties, its consumer loan mix has remained very similar at 77.5% consumer loans and 22% small business loans.

The only red flag that I find is that its net charge-off rate, which measures how much of a lender’s loans are actually lost after recoveries, has increased for its commercial services loans and its small business loans:

Other metrics have not increased, so there is no clear trend in this metric. Regardless, these metrics have proved that AXP’s data advantage has allowed it to extend more loans without increasing the risk of those loans.

I would like to point out that the significant ramp-up of card member loans started in 2021 after the COVID recession, as card member loans have increased an average of 17% every year since. And while all evidence points out that AXP is able to withstand the current risk it has taken on, there is still no evidence of how much a significant economic downturn such as a recession could impact its credit.

Risks

There are also many risks and disadvantages to Amex owning a closed-loop system. For one, because the merchants have to pay high fees for a transaction, low-volume merchants such as small businesses do not accept Amex as a payment option. This, combined with the fact that it has high acquisition costs and slower growth than Visa and Mastercard, led management to open its system to let banks/fintechs issue Amex cards.

Now about one-third of its card issuers are third parties, which significantly scales its product and, in my opinion, reduces its moat. First of all, Amex now has to share the revenue capture with third-party issuers. Amex now has less control over its data, which may not allow it to better understand its customers, provide custom credit allowances, and, most importantly, provide a customized service. Most importantly, its moat is lost, as now the prestige of using the Amex network is somewhat affected. Amex will not be able to charge more for using its network, and it is now more exposed to RTP systems. While Amex has allowed third-party issuers since the 90s by partnering with local banks outside the U.S., this expansion has accelerated in the 2010s with selective partnerships with banks outside of the U.S., such as Scotiabank or Santander. On AXP’s Q1 2022 earnings call, CEO Stephen Squeri said this about its expansion:

“In addition, we're accelerating our focus on fintechs to drive more innovation, including our new partnership with i2c, which will enable fintechs to more seamlessly and quickly issue new products on the American Express network.”

The average discount rate has declined from 2.34% in 2022 to 2.24% in 2025

I think this decline has to do with its expansion into industries and geographies with lower rates and due to the inclusion of third-party issuers that only use its network. However, AXP has more than offset this rate decline with strong billed business growth, as discount revenue and billed business grew 21% and 24%, respectively, over the same period.

It does not seem like the issue is an impact on its growth; rather, it may impact its exclusivity, prestige, and profitability.

Valuation

To value AXP, I projected each of its revenue segments for 5 years and then projected each of the non-cash expenses to reach a free cash flow to equity figure. I do not expect the growth rate of each segment to be significantly different, although I did project it to decrease a few percentage points. Like I have said before, its strong moat allows AXP to be resistant to any short-term pressures, and I do not find any reason why this will change.

To reach an implied price, I assumed an exit EV/EBITDA multiple of 14X. Because of its consistent share buyback program, I projected a 2.5% annual buyback reduction, which can increase the implied price from $400 in 2026 to $445 in 2030.

With an upside of 23% in 2026, I think it is sufficient to justify a Buy being that I basically projected AXP to continue doing the same as before. The projected EPS of $17.57 is very similar to the consensus on Seeking Alpha of 17.59, and, in fact, the consensus for subsequent years is a bit higher than my own projections. This leads me to believe that the market is pricing in a threat or downward pressure on AXP.

Conclusion

To conclude, I find its cardholders to be a moat significantly more powerful than those of its competitors, namely Visa, Mastercard, and Discover (COF). AXP will continue to have a data advantage from its closed-loop system that will allow them to continue providing its top-notch service, offers, and rewards to its customers. These customers will continue to enjoy these perks and spend more than users of other networks. And merchants will continue to accept paying a higher fee even though there are now networks that are practically free to use. With an upside of 23%, it seems like the market is pricing in a risk that can affect the consensus estimates. I think the market is misunderstanding the resilience of its moat and is pricing in the risks the same as it has to its competitors. Although opening its network to third-party issuers can threaten its data advantage, prestige, and control over its environment, there is no clear evidence that it has been affected. Its increase in loan issuance with no other evidence of higher risk means that it has continued to use its data advantage to mitigate its risk in issuing loans. This initiative to scale its network has increased its growth to levels comparable to giants such as Visa, and while it causes AXP to directly compete with RTP systems, there is still no evidence of third-party issuers preferring RTPs over AXP’s network. I do not think the prestige of using the Amex network is affected, because using just the rails is one thing; using the full Amex package is another. I give Amex a Buy.

data.title: Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile: Can The Momentum Continue? (Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Lithium stocks have been on a tear since August 2025 when LCE (lithium carbonate equivalent) prices began to recuperate due to Chinese (CALT) mine stoppage and then gained further momentum when Zimbabwe stopped production (seeking battery integration), and now the Iran war and oil/gas price shocks have provided an even more powerful price demand stimulus: energy security, which comes from wind and solar electricity generation that needs utility-sized storage to increase feasibility and dependency. This is where lithium comes in; it's rapidly becoming a far more strategic metal than the EV evolution promised. Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile (SQM) is one of the top producers and, as I wrote a few days ago regarding Albemarle (ALB), may garner a higher valuation as battery and renewable energy manufacturers look to lock in vital raw materials. In addition, SQM has a relevant fertiliser business that should reap a windfall in the short term as long as the war lasts and beyond.

Operating Model

SQM provides detailed revenue, volume, and gross margins for its business segments, making financial forecasts easier, unlike ALB, which requires more guesswork. On a consolidated basis, revenue should almost double in 2026, driven by LCE prices over 20/kg and a 50% increase in fertiliser sales. Iodine, which accounted for 23% of sales in 2025, may grow by 8%.

Gross margin should increase by over 1000 bp as Lithium jumps from a 26% to 40% level. Note that SQM’s Chile production is subject to a high sales tax of over 34% when LCE prices exceed $10kg, making a higher margin difficult. Fertiliser margins should rise even more from a 15% depressed level to nearly 40%. Thus, EBITDA can more than double, and EPS can increase 200% in 2026.

I assume the LCE price will remain in the $20/kg range through 2028, but will likely be volatile. Fertiliser prices could decline from the peaks, while Iodine is flat at very good margins. Due to increasing global capacity and the JV with Codelco (Chile's copper mine), LCE volumes should grow slowly, reaching 300k tons by 2030. SQM will need to expand capacity in Australia or even Argentina if it wants to keep up with market demand.

Consensus estimates, as shown in the three charts below, are widely dispersed, with many analysts reluctant to assume higher LCE prices.

Valuation

In the past, I valued SQM on an EV/EBITDA basis, but I am now switching to PE because of the JV with Codelco, which will take earnings from 33.5k tons through 2030, and a significant minority interest (balance sheet) that would distort an EV/EBITDA calculation. As with ALB, I'm using a fixed 15x PE to value the shares. As commodity stocks, growth is less relevant than stability, capital discipline, cash flow, and dividend capacity.

The consensus has a similar target PE but on lower EPS, as discussed earlier. My 2026 price target is $106 with LCE prices averaging 20/kg, and I would venture to guess that investors may be inclined to re-rate the lithium producer given the strategic value in energy security.

Peer Comps

I gathered consensus data for many lithium companies, and what first strikes me is that all have breached price targets and appear expensive. However, as mentioned earlier, consensus remains divided on whether LCE prices can stay high or increase from current levels. What is undeniable is that the sector has grabbed market attention after several years in obscurity. This kind of momentum usually lasts 6 to 12 months as the underlying demand scenarios solidify or dissipate.

Risk

The main risk is that the LCE price stops rising and even declines, which could trigger a selling stampede given SQM's momentum. Other risks include regulatory changes in Chile, even under the Codelco JV agreement, that could hurt margins or the value of the Salar. Obsolescence, the evolution of battery technology that could reduce the amount of LCE needed or replace it. Finally, SQM may embark on expensive M&A to offset limited growth in Chile.

Conclusion

I rate SQM a hold and downgrade from buy. While lithium demand should increase and it has become a vital or critical element in energy security, SQM has limited long-term growth due to the Codelco agreement, which caps volumes at 300k tons, and in 2030, it loses control of its main asset (49.9% stake). I prefer ALB long-term in the lithium sector.

data.title: New Record: Stocks Doing Well
data.description:
Well, it has been a while, but the U.S. stock markets continued on their record, upward bounding, tract!

Both the S&P 500 stock index and the NASDAQ index hit new historical highs on Wednesday and were on the way to hit another high on Thursday.

The last time a "new" high was reached for the S&P 500 was on January 27, 2026.

The Dow Jones index hit its last "new" high on February 10, 2026.

The DJIA closed yesterday at 48,464, just 1,724 points below its last historical high, and was above that on Thursday.

In between these dates, stock traders had to deal with what President Trump was generating in the Middle East...the Iran "war".

The important dates here are February 27...a Friday...the day before Trump initiated the "war"...and February 28...the day the "war" began.

Here is a chart showing how the S&P 500 stock index performed during the time between the last two historic highs.

What's going on here?

Well, on January 27, the S&P 500 hit a historical high.

On April 15, the S&P 500 hit a historical high.

What happened?

I believe that market participants had to wait and see how the Federal Reserve was going to respond to these global events.

In December of 2025, the Federal Reserve had just altered its approach to monetary policy. The Fed ended an almost 4-year run of "quantitative tightening" at the close of November, and then in December, it began a run of "quantitative easing."

Participants in the stock market had been "in tune" with the Fed's position during the period of quantitative tightening.

During the early part of the 2020s, the Federal Reserve responded to the disruption caused by the spread of the COVID-19 pandemic and acquired lots and lots of securities to protect the commercial banking system...and the U.S. economy.

The period of quantitative tightening saw the Fed move to remove many of these securities from the Fed's balance sheet and keep bank reserves from being the foundation of a run of more rapid inflation.

At the beginning of December 2025, the Federal Reserve reversed its efforts and began working to build up the amount of securities in the portfolio.

Here is a picture of the Fed's efforts to increase its securities portfolio from the start of December 2025 through April 15, 2026.

Note that from the beginning of December 2025, the Fed was following a policy based just upon how it wanted to build up bank reserves.

After February 28, the Fed not only had to address this issue, but it also had to respond to the Federal Government's needs concerning the financing of the activities going on in the Middle East.

As you can see, the increase in the securities portfolio during this period of time is fairly steady, and the curve rises at a relatively rapid pace. The Fed, during this period, did not just modestly increase the securities.

Also, during this time period, a period of 13 weeks, the M2 money stock increased by just under 2.0 percent. An annual rate of increase of 7.8 percent."

So, the money stock was increasing at a relatively "good" pace.

Growth of the U.S. economy?

Well, the U.S. economy grew by 2.0 percent, year-over-year, in the fourth quarter of 2025, a level it had been at or near for the year 2025.

My estimate of economic growth in the full year is around 2.0 percent.

The economy is staying relatively strong because of the monetary policy of the Federal Reserve.

The central bank scenario?

The Federal Reserve has continued a monetary policy that supports a growth rate in the 2.0 percent to 2.5 percent range, and there is plenty of money available in the banking system.

Economic growth can continue.

Consequently, after leaving the stock market for a while due to concern over what was happening in the Middle East and concern over what might happen as a result of the early Trump economic policies, the tariffs, and such, market players have come back into the marketplace.

As a consequence, the stock market has begun rising again as sufficient economic data are coming into the hands of analysts to convince investors that conditions are right for higher stock prices.

For the economy to "chug" along at around a 2.0 percent pace.

The rise in the stock market from the last banking week of November has been about 1.4 percent. Not much, but in the right direction.

So, the stock market has recovered and is banging against new historical highs this week.

As long as market participants believe that the Federal Reserve is still playing a supportive role here, the stock market will continue to rise.

Of course, we have the Trump-Powell battle ahead of us.

Trump believes in lower interest rates.

Trump wants the Federal Reserve to produce lower interest rates.

This seems to be a necessary part of the story of the Trump II administration.

We'll see how that plays itself out in the coming weeks.

One good thing is that we don't have much time to wait.

It will be a good thing to get this battle behind us.

Unfortunately, the uncertainty surrounding the outcome cannot be dismissed.

How Mr. Warsh is going to perform is a big question. To me, he certainly has the qualities needed for the Chairman's position. And his "history" points further to his capabilities and talent to act.

It doesn't look like it will take a long time to wait before we start getting signals of just how he is working out.

data.title: Gold's Potential Bullish Breakout Above $4,900
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

Gold (XAU/USD) has held steady on Friday, 17 April 2026, during the Asian session with a minuscule intraday gain of 0.2% to trade at $4,800 at this time of writing.

The precious yellow metal was on track for a fourth straight weekly gain, as hopes for a US-Iran peace deal eased fears of stagflation risk and higher longer-term interest rates.

Based on the pre-US-Iran war baseline set on 27 February 2026, gold has plummeted by 22% (high to low) to print an intraday low of $4,099 on 23 March 2026.

Thereafter, it rebounded by 18% to hit an intraday high of $4,871 on Wednesday, 15 April 2026, just below its 50-day moving average, which is acting as an intermediate resistance at around $4,900.

So far, spot gold based on prices quoted by the London Bullion Market Association has underperformed the other global cross-asset classes since the start of the war, where it still recorded a loss of 8% as of Thursday, 16 April 2026, versus positive gains seen in global equities, where the MSCI All Country World Index rebounded back to almost the unchanged level (+0.69%), led by the US mega-cap technology centric, Nasdaq 100 that reversed to a gain of 5.50% (see Fig. 1).

The primary reason for gold to lag equities at this juncture (same movement but different pace) is due to its non-income-bearing feature, as gold does not yield dividends and earnings but competes with the US dollar and fixed-income assets such as bonds.

Intermarket analysis suggests that gold is likely to play a bullish catch-up at this juncture to narrow equities’ outperformance gap.

A less hawkish Fed may put a halt to the US dollar's strength

During the onset of the US-Iran war, the US Dollar Index staged a rally of 3% to hit an 11-month high of 100.64 on 31 March 2026, as Fed funds interest rate cut bets evaporated to a chance of zero in 2026 due to stagflation fear from a potential prolonged global oil supply shock via the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

As ceasefire chances have increased in the past five trading sessions, the CME FedWatch tool, as of 17 April 2026, has started to show an increased odds of a 25 basis point cut (from 0% to 33%), potentially reducing the Fed funds rate to 3.25%-3.50% at the 9 December 2026 FOMC meeting (see Fig. 2).

An implied less hawkish Fed momentary policy priced by the Fed funds futures market has led to a 2.8% drop in the US Dollar Index to 98.20, and it has traded below its 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving averages at the time of writing.

A further weakening of the US dollar is likely to boost another round of a positive feedback loop for gold.

Longer-term US Treasury real yield staged a major bearish reaction below 2.2%

Gold has a significant indirect correlation with the longer-term US Treasury yields, as the precious yellow metal is a non-interest-income-bearing asset.

Hence, a higher 10-year US Treasury real yield (nominal yield minus inflationary expectations from break-even rate) will tend to imply a higher opportunity cost for owning and holding gold, in turn, lesser demand that may drive down prices of gold. Vice versa, gold will tend to benefit from a lower 10-year US Treasury real yield.

The 10-year US Treasury real yield hit a 9-month high of 2.17% on 27 March 2026, just a whisker below its long-term pivotal resistance of 2.20% before it reversed down and broke a key ascending trendline support from the 2 March 2026 low that previously led to a sell-off in gold (see Fig. 3).

Right now, a break below its recent 15 April 2026 low of 1.85% (also the 200-day moving average) is likely to see further weakness in the 10-year US Treasury real yield to retest its medium-term range support at 1.66%, in turn, benefiting gold.

Let us now dissect the short-term outlook (1 to 3 days) of gold (XAU/USD) from a technical analysis perspective.

Gold (XAU/USD) – Poised for a bullish breakout above $4,900

The price actions of gold (XAU/USD) have been traded above its 20-day moving average since a retest of it on Monday, 13 April 2026 (after the failure of the first round of US-Iran peace talks).

Watch the $4,700/4,645 key short-term pivotal support, and a clearance above $4,900 sees the start of another potential bullish impulsive up-move sequence for the next intermediate resistances to come in at $4,980/5,039 and $5,125/5,166 in the first step (see Fig. 4).

However, a break and an hourly close below $4,645 invalidates the bullish tone for a slide towards the next intermediate support at $4,524/4,486 (also 50% Fibonacci retracement of the up move from the 23 March 2026 low to the 15 April 2026 high).

Key elements to support the near-term bullish bias on gold (XAU/USD)

  • Price actions of gold (XAU/USD) have been oscillating within a minor ascending channel since the 23 March 2026 low.
  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator has managed to stage a rebound at a horizontal support of 39.
  • Elliot Wave Theory suggests the recent rally from the 27 March 2026 low of $4,351 is likely considered a minor bullish impulsive wave three structure with its potential terminal zone at $4,980/5,166 (1.382 and 1.618 Fibonacci extensions).

Original Post

data.title: Unity: Too Uncertain To Trust The Turnaround
data.description:
Investment thesis

I recommend holding on to Unity (U). Unity business is in a structurally tough position, even though the last guidance has shown an improvement in the business. The guidance has improved revenue, growing by 17%, and adjusted EBITDA by 58%; however, it is more a short-term restructuring in a battered business than improving the long-term prospects. However, the business is structurally attractive as a networking software company, as the business model is a cash-generating machine. I value the company at $22.76, which represents a -2.5% premium over its current stock price; however, there is only a 49.3% chance of getting a value greater than this current stock price, and those risks are what make me be prudent in my recommendation.

I downgraded Unity’s recommendation from my last article due to the strong advancement of AppLovin (APP) in the market. APP's revenue has reached $1.66 billion in the last quarter. AppLovin controls 80% of the mobile mediation market via MAX. It is a critical market because it gives AppLovin first-party data and bargaining power, and that has created a stronger position in the market at the expense of Unity.

Competition is too intense for Unity to create enough scale to be a relevant player in the long-term

In general, you can see that Unity is facing strong competition on all fronts. From game development tools like Godot and Google's Genie, to intense competition in advertising networking from Meta and AppLovin. In this latter front, it is especially critical to have scale, and it is something Unity is missing compared to direct competitors.

Unity is struggling between their traditional competition, Unreal Engine, and free game development platforms such as Godot. Unreal Engine is more focused on the high-end market and is capturing market share from custom engines developed by major studios. The real competition for Unity is open-source platforms focused on mobile, indie, 2D games like Godot. Even the surge in popularity was due to the strategic blunder created by Unity when the Runtime Fee pricing was introduced. Godot's market share is approaching 15% and going after 38% of Unity’s market share.

Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) has launched a tool, Genie, that can create 3D playable environments from text or image prompts. That is a clear threat to the Unity game simulation platform. When news arose about it, it caused a massive 20% to 26% panic selloff in Unity's stock. I think the reaction was exaggerated. The models Genie generates are probabilistic and are harder to manage than the deterministic ones in Unity. Commercial games need strict physics, repeatable logic, memory optimization, and cross-platform distribution. Those capabilities are the true advantage of Unity.

Meta improved its performance in iOS and positioned its network as a threatening alternative to Unity's, at least so far. However, even when Meta grows in ad presence, Unity benefits indirectly. As Meta pushes prices up, the game developer wins, and Unity, too. The share of total ad revenue from Meta went up from 3.5x to 15x.

AppLovin's competition is especially tough. AppLovin generates 3.3x more revenue than Unity, and it is valued at 15x the market cap. From Q4 2024, AppLovin's revenue exploded, growing more than 50%, while Unity's revenue disappointed, decreasing. In the last quarter, this gap has diminished. In Q4 2025, Unity's revenue growth YoY was 10.1% vs 20.8% from AppLovin. That demonstrates the superiority of MAX and Axon, AppLovin’s ad network, over Vector, Unity’s, at least so far.

Positive margins are easier to achieve

On the margin front, we have two opposing stories. AppLovin faces one of the best operating margins in software with 68.5%, and Unity's is -25.4%. For each $4 in revenue, it loses $1. AppLovin's FCF is operating at a level Unity is too far away from. Free cash flow conversion from capital is 80%-90%, while Unity is in the single digits to low teens. Unity is improving from -1.07% in Q1 2024 to 8.44% in Q4 2025. However, it is not even at a fraction of the level I would like to see in a software, network-based business model.

Unity doesn't buy back its shares, and stock-based compensation has been out of control from 2014-2024. Last year, with the new management team, the company decreased stock-based compensation below free cash flow (Figure 4), but still at a high level. SBC expense is around 80% of free cash flow, which means that most of the free cash flow that should go to investors is going to employees. And that is stock-based compensation at the grant date. I estimate a real SBC cost, at exercise, at $1 billion, calculated as an increase of 23 million shares at $44.17 per share.

Despite high SBC, in my opinion, I see that management is tackling the problem. SBC has decreased from 91% in 2023-2024 to 73% in 2025, and more is to come. Unity granted 206,244 stock options in 2025, down from 3,187,067 in 2024, a ~94% reduction. The company has changed to a performance-based restricted stock incentive program for some executives, dependent on achieving revenue and EBITDA goals.

Unity's business model as a software network provider is highly cash-generative. The company has a negative operating margin of -19.45% in the last quarter, -$97.8 million, but it is influenced by high depreciation and amortization of $131.9 million, much of which is around the amortization of ironSource intangibles, an accounting charge without cash consequences, and something already priced in. If we get rid of any accounting items, we can arrive at a cash margin of $135.4, a solid 26.9%.

Valuation

After the new guidance management was released on March 26, 2026, I expect an accelerated growth rate in Unity's revenue. Total strategic revenue, excluding non-core revenue, and an insight into future performance grew 34% YoY, with strategic growth at 48% YoY. In that line, the new agreement with Meta VR must be inside this guidance. That is always good news, and the market made the share rise 9% after the news. I am always skeptical because this is a win for the company, but it is more difficult to identify the losses. So, it is hard to know the net effect of wins and losses.

The company is going to present Q1 2026 earnings results on May 7, and I see a solid 17% growth rate in revenue to reach $506 million, in line with management's expectations. I project adjusted EBITDA for the next quarter in line with management's guidance. With this, I assume management turnaround is getting traction, at least in the short term.

However, long-term expectations are dominated by the intense competition either in the ad network business or in the game developer engine market. I foresee a 15% growth rate in the next three years as the Grow segment accelerates its growth rate, mainly as the company gets rid of its non-strategic businesses, such as ironSource Ads Network and Supersonic, its game publishing label. From 2029 to 2032, the growth rate will decrease to 10%. The Vector adoption curve flattens, and AppLovin's structural advantage erodes growth. At the same time, the Create segment suffers from being in the middle of low-cost open-source alternatives and high-end Unreal Engine. Finally, from 2032, Unity faces structural market penetrations, and it grows as the broader organic growth in the 3D interactive and gaming market at 5%.

Unity business is a complex one that has two very different models, Create and Grow. Both are networked businesses that require different drivers. The first one is the game development platform. Unity needs to have an extended developer community. As more developers develop more games for the platform, more users are attracted, more earnings for those developers, and the networked business grows larger. With the Runtime Fee trouble, the cycle was broken in favor of Godot. The second network is the advertising platform business. The company needs a large base of developers and games to sell to advertising agencies. As the number of games developed on the platform is low, the capacity to attract agencies is lower. Therefore, the main driver in this business is the number of games developed.

In just a sentence: in a networked business, revenue and costs are highly inversely correlated. However, if the company doesn’t get enough revenue due to competition, margins won’t increase accordingly. I estimate the cash margin will increase by four percentage points in the coming years, from 24% to 32% , making the business healthier than having clear leadership in the market. That will stabilize margins at 30% from 2033, with no further improvement.

I estimate stock-based compensation will decrease to $300 billion because the stock price has decreased, and will continue to decline in the coming years. I don’t expect a significant additional decrease in SBC expense, as the program usually lasts three to four years. Management has transformed the program, so I expect a reduction to $100 million from 2029. It is hard to predict how much the SBC expense would be, but as this management stays, the company will have a more reasonable expense.

My value estimation, once I run the discounted cash flow method for the 10-year period, is 22.76, a -2.5% premium over the current stock price. Practically in line with market valuation. At a multiple of 24x price-to-free cash flow, it is a fairly valued software company yet to prove leadership in its market. Free cash flow yield is 4.2%, in line with the 10-year bond, with much growth potential, as this yield can reach 9.6% in five years.

My valuation is based on a short-term recovery to make the business healthier, but a challenging industry in the long term that will prevent Unity from being one of the leaders in the market. If the company beats AppLovin and keeps being the leader in the game engine platform, the value will be far higher. On the contrary, if the company is not able to regain the trust of the developers' community, Unity won't launch enough games to support the advertising business, and the revenue will be more modest. That will decrease its stock price to $12.53, which is the 5th percentile in the risk model developed in the next section.

Risks

I consider the valuation to have significant uncertainties, as the company is in the process of a turnaround. I consider revenue growth can swing from 25% to 5% in the next few years, with the same difference around the baseline in the coming years. And cash margin varies five percentage points around the baseline. This kind of business has demand and supply scale effects inversely correlated with revenue and margin, multiplying risks.

Running those variables and uncertainties in my Monte Carlo simulation gives me the probability of getting a value greater than the current stock prices is 49.3%. That tells me that uncertainty is total and everything can happen. So the valuation is meaningless with a clear direction.

Complete analysis and valuation

Conclusion

I consider Unity to be exposed to high risks. It is accomplishing a deep restructuring, and I think in the right direction. However, it operates in a difficult industry with strong players. In my base case, I think it is fairly priced, and the market is betting on a short-term turnaround, but with difficulties establishing clear leadership in its industry, in the game engine market, and in the ad network business. I see a value of $22.76 per share, but with a probability of being higher than the current stock price at 49.3%, considering all the uncertainties, which is a high uncertainty. With all of that, I recommend holding onto the stock.

data.title: Rates Spark: Expectation Management
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

Some risk assets have more than fully retraced, but rates are right to remain concerned about inflation

Markets continue to take the glass-half-full approach to the situation in the Middle East. One could easily argue that valuations start to look vulnerable here. After all, some market segments have already fully retraced the drawdowns caused by the turmoil.

At the same time, the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed for now. As time passes and expectations are not met, real knock-on effects could start to be felt clearly – after all, economies are living off reserves.

In the background are stories such as the IEA warning that European airlines have maybe six weeks before they run out of jet fuel, or the IMF again warning that the economic damage is being underestimated.

The ECB minutes, meanwhile, confirmed the central bank’s hawkish shift. Still, it was signalled again that there is unlikely to be enough data available at the upcoming meeting in two weeks' time to change the policy stance.

Market pricing for the April meeting has continued to slide, with only 3bp of tightening now in the forward rate. With an implied probability of just over 10%, an April hike scenario has effectively been reduced to a tail risk.

Further out, though, the market pricing did bounce off from a low of seeing just 50bp of tightening by year-end. For one, oil prices have moved a little higher towards US$100/bbl again. But we also think the significance of the 50bp might rest on the 'one is none' narrative.

While this is a fair assumption in the sense that you want a policy shift to be effective, our own revised base case scenario actually sees only one rate hike. Of course, this remains highly path-dependent on the energy situation, but we think it is a plausible scenario if one views the ECB’s strategy through the lens of expectations management and the ECB’s concerns around the credibility of its reaction function.

We think rates markets are right not to look through the inflation story as readily as some other market segments. Keep in mind that the containment of longer rates, absent a notable rise in growth concerns, is premised on the reaction function that the market is pricing on the front end. That is a delicate balance the ECB needs to manage, and also leaves the long end still prone to temporary rises.

Friday’s events and market view

Despite the growing optimism in some corners of riskier asset markets, investors are still looking to the Middle East, especially ahead of the weekend.

In terms of data, the week will end on a quiet note, with the eurozone trade balance for February the only release of interest. Central bankers will still be speaking, with some focus this time on the Bank of England, with Sarah Breeden and Chief Economist Huw Pill speaking.

From the Fed, Mary Daly, Thomas Barkin and Christopher Waller will be speaking. But overall, markets should already have a clearer picture of central banks’ thinking after the busy communications over the past few days.

Primary markets are also quiet with no scheduled bond supply for the day. Rating agencies have pencilled in review dates for the Netherlands (S&P), Belgium and the EFSF (Moody’s), as well as Italy, Slovakia and the EU (DBRS).

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
